perturbation.original_text: Any payment required to be made by Sponsor hereunder that is not paid within fourteen (14) days from the date such payment becomes due and owing shall bear interest at an annual rate of twelve percent (12%) per annum or, if lower, the maximum allowed by law from the due date to the date payment is actually made.
perturbation.changed_text: Any payment required to be made by Sponsor hereunder that is not paid within fourteen (14) days from the date such payment becomes due and owing shall bear interest at an annual rate of twenty-five percent (25%) per annum from the due date to the date payment is actually made.
perturbation.explanation: Charging 25% interest per annum violates California's usury laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution Article XV Section 1 sets the maximum interest rate for loans and forbearance at 10% per annum, unless there is an exemption (e.g. licensed broker). The modified text specifies an interest rate of 25%, which exceeds the legal limit and contradicts California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - California Code of Regulations This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies an interest rate of 25%, which exceeds the legally permissible limit for interest rates as explained in the Law Explanation, directly contradicting California law related to usury. This falls under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction because the changed text sets a requirement that violates state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. Although the scraped snippets are not helpful, the Law Explanation clearly states the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All notices, requests, or offers required or permitted to be made under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed properly delivered on the earlier of actual receipt or three days after the date deposited in the U.S. Mail, by certified mail, return receipt requested, or by recognized overnight delivery service with signature required (e.g., FedEx, UPS) addressed as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices, requests, or offers required or permitted to be made under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed properly delivered five days after the date deposited in the U.S. Mail, by standard mail service.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the delivery method to standard mail with a 5-day delivery window contradicts California law regarding timely notice for legal and contractual matters.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 specifies how service by mail is considered complete and allows for extensions of time based on distance and method. The modified text removes requirements for certified mail or overnight service (methods that ensure quicker receipt and proof of delivery) and creates a flat, longer delivery window, potentially conflicting with requirements for time-sensitive notices and filings under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 regarding service by mail. The original text requires certified mail or overnight delivery, ensuring quicker receipt and proof of delivery. The changed text uses standard mail with a 5-day delivery window. CCP 1013(a) specifies that service is complete at the time of deposit but extends the response time by five calendar days if both mailing and address are within California. The modified text alters the legally recognized procedure for service by mail, potentially impacting time-sensitive legal notices, creating an inconsistency, as defined in the instructions, regarding time-sensitive obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly after the receipt by an indemnified party of notice of any claim, such indemnified party will, if a claim with respect thereto is to be made against an indemnifying party, give such indemnifying party written notice within a reasonable period of such asserted liability or commencement of such action or proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: The indemnified party is not required to notify the indemnifying party of any claim unless a lawsuit is filed, and then the indemnified party has complete discretion as to when, or if, such notice is provided.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating the requirement for timely notice contradicts California law regarding duty to defend and fair opportunity to respond to claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code Section 554 implies a duty to notify insurers (and by extension, indemnifying parties) within a reasonable timeframe to allow them to investigate and defend claims. The changed text removes any requirement to provide notice of claims unless a lawsuit is filed, potentially hindering the indemnifying party's ability to mount a defense and violating California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, INS 554 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Insurance Code - INS DIVISION 1. GENERAL RULES GOVERNING INSURANCE [100 - 1879.8] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) PART 1. THE CONTRACT [100 - 679.75] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) CHAPTER 6. Loss [520 - 580] ( Chapter 6 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) ARTICLE 3. Notice and Proofs of Loss [550 - 557.5] ( Article 3 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) 554. Delay in the presentation to an insurer of notice or proof of loss is waived, if caused by an act of his, or if he omits to make objection promptly and specifically upon that ground. (Enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text eliminates the requirement for timely notice to the indemnifying party unless a lawsuit is filed, giving the indemnified party complete discretion on whether to provide notice at all. This contradicts the principle outlined in the Law Explanation that indemnifying parties should receive timely notice to investigate and defend claims. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (INS 554) focuses on insurers and waiving delay due to the insurer's actions, the Law Explanation extends the duty to notify to indemnifying parties, arguing that hindering their ability to mount a defense violates California law. The original text mandated written notice within a reasonable period, whereas the modified text allows for no notice until a lawsuit, which could significantly impair the indemnifying party's ability to investigate and prepare a defense. This delay is a critical contradiction.
